speaker: interviewer
text: Max Epstein, March 10, 1995. OK. We were talking about your experiences in Auschwitz. And we talked about Leib. And I want to pick up a little bit with-- you were talking a little bit about the selections. I want to know how that occurred, and how they determined those kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as Leib is concerned, my dream would have been to find him and bring him to Yad Vashem and make him one of the righteous. That's what I was dreaming. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyway, selections. Every Sunday in the regular labor camp that I was in, they would select the Jews. And usually, they would bring people-- I'm not proud what I'm going to say now. You know, we like always to see ourselves-- we-- we do rewrite history. I think it would be unfair to this project if I was going to sanitize it, even for sake of anybody, including probably myself. So what I'm going to say probably is not always what I would like. I know it's probably published. And I know it's publicized. It's a public event. And I prefer not-- but I think that in order to-- to pay tribute to those who did not survive, I think it behooves me to be not only candid, but truthful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Selections were terrible. Every Sunday, you knew you were going to go. One of those selections, I had to beg for life, and got away because I had a good accent, which impressed the block command. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Another one was, for example, they would bring all-- there were a lot of Hungarians. This was the Hungarian deportation period. That's where they brought 400,000 or more-- I don't know the numbers-- of Hungarian Jews during the summer of 1944 to Auschwitz. And most of them were liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a lot of Hungarians were coming around. And they were already starving. They already looked terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, just to prove to Leib if he ever listens to it-- he's probably dead-- to tell him that I did make it, that I learn a little bit, that I was not that naive, because he thought that I would be just eliminated in no time. He looked at me like a little kid who-- you know, who just cannot survive. And maybe he was right. But anyway, what I did, for example, they used to bring you in a line, put you in a line and march you round, round, round. And if somebody in front would look at you, I would pick a terribly looking fellow, very tall, and stand behind him, because as they say, go, go, go, go, they'll pick him. And I'll go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a very pretty statement to make. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Am I to apologize for that? I don't know. When you fight for survival, that's not a pretty scene. It's not the same as being in the army and helping your buddy, you know. This is not the same thing. So it's-- did I condemn him in front of me? Probably not. I mean, he would have-- just, I don't know. This is probably an excuse of every [SIGHS] scoundrel anyway. But-- but I don't think that that is the case.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, just to give you another flavor of what the situation of a selection would look like. One of the selections when I was selected, I was still in the hospital. He asked me what religion. There was no religious life. There was nothing religious about the whole place. And there was nobody-- I never saw anybody there religious or being involved in a religion. But I remember one event, which was very interesting. The day when I was selected to be eliminated in the hospital and the French physician was trying to remove me from the list-- or the young people that he wanted to remove, not only myself. I don't know how many he removed. In a fact, after they came with the trucks, everybody who remained was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emotionally-- you know, survived. Look, I was just facing death and I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a guy, I'm sure a very assimilated Jew, maybe from Latvia, I think, spoke only German, I think, suddenly stood up. I think he was, in a fact, maybe even somebody very highly educated. Somebody even mentioned that he was a faculty member at some university or something. He stood up, turned to the east side, and started praying, whatever he remembered. And somebody pointed out to me, and says, look. He's turning to religion. Well, you know, it's a weak time, you know? So it's-- religion is, after all, does serve the purposes. I'm not supposed to be lecturing on religion. Anyway, just-- you asked me about the religious events. And that's all I have seen, frankly. I haven't seen any religious activity. Did you ever give up hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. As a matter fact, the last few weeks in Auschwitz-- as I told you, I was a little punk who told my mother to give up, hoping for-- stop crying, because it's no use. So, obviously, I wasn't seeing this as a surviving-- as a place-- Leib himself was telling me, look, he says, neither you or I are going to survive that, so forget it. Don't worry about it. He said, I'll probably wind up in the Russian front or something. You was already there once. He says, I probably never make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yes, I-- the mood was-- and my friend taking a thermometer in a hot water in order to commit suicide in a modern way was just an indication of time. And in the last few weeks in Auschwitz, there were weeks, there were days, where I thought that it would be just as well if I just-- if I only had the courage to take that thermometer, or whatever means to do that. Yes, I felt that-- but there were days, of course, which were different.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, yes, I was-- I had a big scar here on my thing, which never healed until I-- the war ended, and I got food and you know-- I got good food. And then, of course, it naturally healed. But I have had a scar here where I was beaten by somebody into the mud. And I got terrible pus here and I couldn't move. As a matter of fact, during the time when they took us out from Auschwitz, you know, they had selection, who is going to be shipped out, and they want to make sure that you have any diseases, so, you know, like all kinds of infectious disease. They would make you lower the pants to see whether you have any, you know, pimples or anything on your body. And I lowered them right up to here, up to the knee. If I have lowered it below knee, I would-- I would obviously be selected not to go to another camp, in other words, to leave Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yes, I would say that Auschwitz was probably the closest that I have been to being completely-- I presume my mother was not alive anymore. And I- I didn't really see that as- as-- as a way of going on. And so, yes, that probably was the most critical time, where I really was ready to kind of, you know, yes or no. And did you have any other incidences of brutality on you, inflicted on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, except the beating at one place-- well, you know, you ever-- I watched a movie a few days ago with somebody about sheep farmers. Did you ever see sheep, how they are being sent? I mean, did you ever see any time anything that has to do with large number of people, sheep or no sheep? I shouldn't use the word sheep because that has been a very bad connotation by some people who have related to that-- referred to that, and I don't think it's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, any time you deal with the masses of people, what does it mean, beating? I mean, you run. And there are somebody standing with a club and hitting you. And if you are smart enough, you move in the right direction-- I- I don't know-- and you're alert.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes you miss it and you get it. Sometimes you don't. By and large-- so, yeah, people were beaten all the time around me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when I've been beaten physically myself? Not as many times. I probably wouldn't survive. Even one of them just got me practically out of the picture. So, no. No, not beaten every day and every time. But-- but people were beaten around me and they were all masses. You have to understand that you're living in a mass of people with the smell and the dirt and all of that around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're sleeping with 8, 10 people in a bunk, you know. You are-- you are all dead together and it's all a mass. To come back and start looking at it as an individual is bordering on bizarre. There is no such a thing. You are a part of a jelly that is around you. And whatever happens, happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after Auschwitz? How did you-- how did they determine where you would be going next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were picking people to send into Germany, to camps. Auschwitz already was under-- the Russians were crossing the rivers in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: End of October, beginning of November. I lost track.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And Russians were already approaching center of Poland, so they were liquidating Auschwitz slowly. And they were shipping people out to the other places. And, of course, as the war get from bad to worse for the Germans, they were looking for more labor obviously, forced labor. So they were shipping out. And so I got in one of those shipments.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wound up in Sachsenhausen. Actually, it was a combination of two. I went-- that's next to Berlin. Sachsenhausen is-- I- I will just spend one minute on describing-- you asked me the question-- Mike asked me the question about-- your name is Mike, right? That's why I remember. Mike asked me the question about who governed these places? And in camps, there was internal prisoner government, not that the Germans weren't around you all the time, I mean the SS. But-- but Auschwitz was Polish. There was no question about it. Everybody in power there was Polish. The future prime minister of Poland, Cyrankiewicz, was there later on, after World War II.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when you moved to Sachsenhausen, it was German. Those are the Germans, the liberals, Social Democrats, Communists, whoever got sucked into that place by the Nazis were-- or thrown into that by the Nazis, were-- Sachsenhausen was typically German. And it was a pleasure to be there. It was well-organized, very liberal. Nobody beat you. I came in with my bad leg. They sent me to a clinic. They bandaged it. There was a physician, a prisoner, who took care of it. And I was impressed. Now, I hear today the horror story about Sachsenhausen, [LAUGHS] I says, my God, horrors has its degrees. Apparently, it was horror. But it was nothing compared to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there I was a few weeks only. I stopped-- first they sent us to some big hangar in [INAUDIBLE], which is a small town next to-- actually, not a small town. It's a suburb of Berlin. And then to Sachsenhausen, which is not far.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, every night the British and the Americans were coming to bomb. Every 4:00 o'clock in morning, you could set a clock on it. And they put us-- and they throw us out from the barracks in order to go to the little field-- to the little forest there so they don't bomb us. And I was praying that they bomb us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't know this was really an irony. They were worried about our survival? I don't-- couldn't understand it. Maybe as a-- as a labor for them. But, anyway, so we stayed there for a few weeks. And what did you do in Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Nothing whatsoever, sitting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, we went to a camp which was in the Black Forest. It's west of Bavaria, and-- that's where the Black Forest is. And that was a camp in the mountains that they built, which I found out later, although we had some inclination what it is, that was the-- the V-2 bomb. The V-2 were bombed out. I think that was next to Berlin were the plants and they were bombed. So they moved them, and build them in the mountains. Inside the mountains, they were actually carving out whole areas under the mountains. This is a very rocky area. And that's what-- the process was very, very simple. Every day, they would take 100 people. And the man in charge of the 100 people, which is called in Italian kapo, I don't know if you heard this name, this is taken out. And he was in charge of that group. And his responsibility was to bring 10 dead, so to prove, that, you know, that there was work done. And so there was very little survival in that-- in that particular camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It was called Dora Two or something. It was a-- given a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- well, I went to a couple of these things. The first day I went in is they picked us up to pick up a train-- unload a train of clothing that came from Siberia-- not from Siberia, from the Russian front, which was actually very padded [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clothing for Russian pilots or Russians-- you know, they had very heavily padded, beautiful uniforms. The first thing I did is got into the truck-- into the railroad car what I was supposed to help clean out into the whatever we do. And I dressed myself up. I had beautiful, warm clothes, nice shoes. And that was my first day. And then I continued sent-- be sent to one of those every morning to some of those groups to work. And I saw that that survival was very slim. I wasn't beaten myself exactly. But I knew that this is a question of statistics, you know, If you go like that for a month, you're not going to come back. And, somehow or other-- I don't remember the details. I wish I did. There was a German Jew. I think he was a Jew. He was a German. That camp, by the way, was primarily run by Russians. And the reason is that the Nazis never recognized the Geneva Convention vis-a-vis the Russian army. So they stuck all the Russians in concentration camps, as opposed to Americans and British, which went to stalags. And as a result, this camp was run by Russians, most of them Russian POWs. And this particular guy was a German, but-- he spoke German very well, must have been a German Jew. I don't know why, but he-- I think he had got a lot of influence, because he spoke German very well, and maybe got buddy-buddies with the management of the camp. I don't know the details. I know that my head of my-- sorry-- my head of my barrack is-- was a musician, a German musician, don't know if a Jew or not. I can't say. Probably not. And the head of the camp, despite the fact it was POWs, Russian POWs run mostly by Russian, the top man was a German. And I remember that one day I was-- you know, when you passed him, you had to take off your hat. And I took my hat off. And he walked up to me, and says, I'm a prisoner. You don't take a hat off. So he must have been some sort of a Social Democrat German. And, anyway, this guy managed to get me in the kitchen as a potato peeler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stayed most of the time there. And I survived because of that. Most of them were Russians, the potato peelers. And one of them was Serge Pipof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Serge Pipof. Young boy, 17 years old. He became my best friend. And I took him after the war to the collection center for Russian refugees, which I blame myself for to this very day. But I'll get to that back if we have enough tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Dora?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Til beginning of April, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. The head of it was a pilot, a Russian pilot. He was the head of the potato peelers. And I remember that he would turn to me-- he always liked me very much-- and discuss with me Russian literature. And would turn to his fellow Russians and say-- and they knew I was a Jew. At that time, I was still-- later on, I did not. I was-- I hid my Jewish-- this was-- affiliation. But at that time, I still-- it was known that I was a Jew. And I had the proper sign for it. And he said to them, look at that. He is a bloody Jew with whom I can discuss Russian literature, and you bums don't know even anything about. At the end of the stay there, I once turned to them, and wanted to know-- he was very crude, in some sense, but he was very well-educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked him, how come you didn't mind my being Jewish? And he says, my wife is Jewish. So it explained a little bit, because another guy there, he was from the south of Russia, he was a pathological anti-Semite. But he liked me. So he decided that he's going to fix my leg. I had another problem with my leg from marching. And it didn't-- didn't heal, because apparently at the edges, if you don't cut out the-- this flesh that is bad, it will never close. So he says, I got here a Russian physician in the clinic, in the camp. I'll take you to him. He's going to fix you up. And he took me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the way when we were walking-- I forgot-- Nikolai was his name, Mike. He used to tell me, look, Max, he says, when the-- when the war is over, the Germans won't kill all the Jews. We're going to finish them. Now, he's talking to me. And I'm a Jew. He was so pathologically anti-Semitic that he couldn't even separate that discussion from the fact he was talking to a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, I cannot obviously-- certainly, he was no great philosopher either. But, apparently, I'm just trying to show the relationship between people, and what kind of friends you pick up, and enemies, [LAUGHS] and in between. He was also responsible for fixing my leg. And Serge, of course, remained my very, very close friend for the rest of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by time of the liquidation of Dora, because the Americans were coming in, I could-- I hear the tanks. I could hear the fire. And we were trying to run. And for four days, we were marched to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Dora.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Dora. And the four days-- since I think I was asked the question here, who is an SS and who isn't, and are there any nice SS? Certainly not who volunteered to SS. But I was in a group that was marched. And by that time, they were already mobilizing the old men. This guy must have been over 50, and marched next to me-- not marched. I mean, he was in charge of the column we were marching. And they were shooting people. They were killing. The young SS were running around. If somebody was dragging or something, they-- they will kill him. And I remember we came across a dead horse. And everybody jumped on it and started grabbing the meat and eat. It was unbelievable. I learned from the French prisoners how to eat dandelions. It's a French delicacy, raw dandelions. It's something else. I learned from the Russian how to wash in snow. Take your clothes and roll in snow. And you get red like a lobster. And you feel wonderful. Of course, it's terrible to do [LAUGHS] it in the beginning. I remember that I came to - to Buchenwald-- at the beginning, when I was stuck in in a place, and they told us in the middle of the winter-- that was the beginning in Dora, when they made us dig channels. And I was standing in water and it was half-frozen. And I was standing like that. And just-- and a Russian walked up to me, and said, move, idiot, or you will die. [LAUGHS] And it always remained with me, you move. No matter how bad you feel, you move. And so I learned a lot about-- from different cultures, different people. It was-- it wasn't interesting at the time. I couldn't care less. But when I look in retrospect, I saw different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I marched next to this German officer, which-- with an SS uniform. And he said to me-- at one point, he asked me to carry his rucksack, which I did. And I ask him, why is it so heavy? And he says, civilian clothes, you idiot. So it's obviously what he had in mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one point, I decide I'm going to run. I saw the American tanks in the distance. I said, I'm going to take-- to run for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a Russian uniform I traded, I think, for bread or something from a Russian prisoner and a Russian overcoat. And I decided to run. And I started running.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German guy says to me, you'll be back. And I says, no. I'm not going to be back. I'm going. So he said, OK, good luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started running. And, of course, in the forest outside of the open field, there were sitting the young German, what they call it, the home guard or whatever they were. And they were shooting with machine guns. I was back. And he says to me, you see? I told you, you will be back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so anyway-- different people, different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we got to Buchenwald. In Buchenwald they-- Buchenwald was Czechish-run, wonderful place. I mean, the first thing they ask us is, who was cruel? Who was dead? Who should be liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was very interesting. I really fell in love with the Czechs. That's why I stayed after the war in Prague. And I thought that they were really the most liberal, and the most civilized people of all the Eastern populations that I knew, again, statistically speaking. Some of my best friends are everywhere. By the way, I never raised a hand at another German. I was in Czechoslovakia after the war and they asked me to participate in beating Germans. I would never raise a hand against a German, because I would never know whether one of them could be Leib. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it still applies to this very day. So we should never make any-- anti-Semitism is exactly what it is. It's classification of a-- whether right or wrong. It's always grouping. People should never fall into that-- that trap, ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway-- so in Buchenwald, they told us to-- all the Jews come out. And, of course, Serge said to me, are you crazy? Of course, when I came to the new camp, they ask you for your name. And I adopted my name [? Grad, ?] which is my mother's maiden name. At Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: AT Buchenwald. And so Serge says to me, are you crazy? What are you guys talking about? They are going to shoot you. And, indeed, I was wrong. And he was wrong, because it turned out that the Germans were getting very itchy about the fact that some of-- Buchenwald-- you know that Buchenwald, the-- the German commander of Buchenwald got an order to kill all the prisoners. And he refused, just like Paris burning, you know, the same kind of event in a concentration camp. You know, the commander of Paris refused to burn Paris. This commander refused to burn-- to kill prisoners in Buchenwald. And so, as a result, all then were-- all those were sent to Theresienstadt, which at that time became a paradise. They brought the Red Cross and they wanted to show the Jews are still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I missed that opportunity [LAUGHS] and I went with Serge in a most deadly, I think that probably one of the worst killings were really going on after that, when I was herded into trains and we were killed like sardines. But there were no Jews anymore left. They were Frenchmen, and Poles, and Russians primarily, and some German. And they killed you in the cars at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were actually parking us inside a railroad track. That is subsequent from after I was sent to Mauthausen, which is on the way for two weeks through Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Wait. You're going from Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going from Buchenwald to Leitmeritz, which is a concentration camp next to Theresienstadt, but not-- no Jews in it. And there we stayed for about two weeks. And then they put us on a railroad track to go to Mauthausen. Theresienstadt is-- and Leitmeritz is in Sudetenland. It's the north part-- northwest corner of Czechoslovakia. Mauthausen is in Austria, southwest corner of Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going down through Prague in a railroad train. And they would put us in an open coal cars, 120 people in each car, with two Germans sitting on top. And at night, they would put us in one of those-- sort of like a little-- you know, when you have sometimes train going through a depression or a place where the railroads are below the rest of the landscape, you know, like in the station in Winnetka, to make the comparison. So what they do, they would sit on top and they will shoot into the trains, just for fun of it. So people were dying and we were throwing out the bodies. By the time we were finished, I think we were 30. And Serge was with me all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once in awhile, when the train passed, a Czechish worker would walk with his lunch. And he would throw the lunch into the car. 100 people will jump on that one. I had a bad knee. I couldn't move. We were already insane with hunger and thirst. There was no water, no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that Serge would go into the crowd to fight for the bread. And all he could get is whatever his palm of his hand could grab out of that sandwich, you know? So he would bring it like this, that piece of thing back. And he would come next to me. We would divide it in half.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's courage, not speaking of devotion, but courage, because you're so hungry. You're insane with hunger. So, again, another description of what-- there were a lot of bright sparks in my experience. And I choose to remember the sparks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help any people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I failed. There was one event what I failed. I would not want to discuss it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ones that you did help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. Probably-- whether I brought my boss to a job, or whether I had some food [SIGHS] that I could share. Once in a while I worked in a-- in peeling potatoes. We'd steal potatoes together with the Russians. But then I got in a clique with the Russians. And I didn't help somebody I should have helped, because I was afraid that the Russians will, you know, disown me, or whatever. Look, it's not an easy-- this is not-- you know-- you know, I don't have the luxury-- I didn't have the luxury, neither age-wise, experience-wise-- remember I was 19 years old-- that I could afford this contemplation in-- in such a terrible environment as that. I just-- I'm trying to excuse myself. No, I didn't do anything terrible. I have a clear conscious. I've never done anything. I have probably been a good friend, and a loyal-- a loyal comrade. But you wish you did more. There were instances which I wish back I did more. I did Serge too in, not willingly. After reading Solzhenitsyn, I know where he wound up. And I was the-- I was the-- the-- I was the [SIGHS] Count Bezukhov vis-a-vis my poor Russian peasant I was playing. I don't want to play the game. I was-- I loved him. But I said to him, look, you are a stupid peasant. You don't understand anything. I am the educated person. I read Marx, you know. I know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says to me, Max, you realize you have a number, at least. When I come back to Russia, they will shoot me. I says, what are you talking about? This is the wonderful country, just won the war. It's such a wonderful, you know, place, you know, the anti-fascist and all these things. And they helped and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't realize that he knew what he was talking about and I didn't know what the hell I was talking about. They came to his village, the Germans. They took the whole family out and shot everybody. As far as he was concerned, he was in the same position that I was. He lost his extended family as well. And so, certainly-- and he knew what he was talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the other hand, he was-- yeah, he wasn't educated, but so what? Education doesn't buy-- I-- I never found anybody like that. Well, no-- sorry, take it back. I did find-- We have to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he remains very vivid.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pick up here. We're going to--
